english: Sesame Soup Noodles with Egg and Sausage

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hong Kong sesame soup noodles; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM for noodle-based soup dishes used as closest parent; no specific Hong Kong sesame soup noodle entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Hong Kong sesame soup noodles as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Hong Kong sesame soup noodles weighing approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, sesame broth, egg, corn, sausage, and fresh carrots.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles in soup, mixed ingredients — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables for sesame-broth noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hong Kong sesame soup noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 20121), sesame paste/tahini (FDC ID 12166), hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 01129), corn (FDC ID 11167), pork sausage (FDC ID 07065), and carrot (FDC ID 11124), weighted to a representative bowl composition: approximately 55% noodles, 20% broth with sesame paste, 8% egg, 7% corn, 5% sausage, 5% carrot. Sodium elevated to reflect broth contribution. Values represent a per-100g average of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat-based, boiled — GI reference from University of Sydney GI Database; sesame paste contribution negligible to GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hong Kong sesame soup noodles. GI of 52 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, low category). Sesame paste has a very low GI (~40) and constitutes a minor proportion of the dish; egg, corn, sausage, and carrot contributions are minor. The dominant glycemic driver is the wheat noodle component. GL per serving calculated as: (GI 52 × carbohydrate per serving 63.2 g) / 100 = 32.9. Carbohydrate per serving = 15.8 g/100g × 400 g serving = 63.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sesame broth
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (1,680 mg per bowl), this dish provides a substantial portion of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium broth or consume only a portion of the soup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 52 (low) but a glycemic load of approximately 32.9 per standard bowl, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and saturated fat from sausage
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat contribution from sausage (approximately 0.9 g saturated fat per 100 g) are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; choosing a lower-sodium broth and replacing sausage with a leaner protein would improve the cardiovascular profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hong Kong Sesame Soup Noodles is a warm, comforting noodle dish originating from Hong Kong Chinese culinary tradition and popular in Singapore's casual dining scene. The dish features wheat-based noodles served in a fragrant sesame-enriched broth, typically made by blending sesame paste or tahini into a light chicken or pork stock to create a rich, nutty, and slightly creamy soup base. Standard toppings include a soft-boiled or hard-boiled egg, sweet corn kernels, sliced Chinese sausage (lap cheong) or pork sausage, and julienned or sliced fresh carrots, providing a balance of textures ranging from silky noodles to crunchy vegetables. The sesame broth imparts a distinctive toasted, nutty aroma and a mildly savoury flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 118 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 4.1 g fat, and 15.8 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy meal. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), driven primarily by the wheat noodle component, though the glycemic load per standard 400 g bowl is approximately 32.9, which is clinically significant for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to the broth, making this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains sesame, gluten, and egg allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians due to the sausage component. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at noodle shops and casual Chinese eateries, and is associated with Hong Kong-style comfort food.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hong Kong sesame soup noodles was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Nutrient values were estimated by component-level calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients weighted to a representative bowl composition. GI was estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles as the dominant glycemic driver. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach rather than a direct measurement of the assembled dish.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the component ingredients in the sources used. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in the ingredient components per USDA SR Legacy data. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from sesame paste and egg components per USDA SR Legacy. The dish is served at The Noodle Place - Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 7.90. Halal status is uncertain as the sausage type is not specified; assumed non-halal due to likely pork sausage content. The GI category is low (GI 52 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold). Glycemic load per serving = (52 × 63.2) / 100 = 32.9, where 63.2 g carbohydrate = 15.8 g/100g × 400 g serving size.
